ontology.foodex2_description: Baklava (filo pastry with nuts and syrup)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated baklava code; A0EKN (sweet pastry) is the closest defensible parent category in the EFSA FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for baklava; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: rose water or orange blossom water

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of baklava is one diamond- or square-cut piece weighing approximately 30 grams, as commonly portioned in Turkish and Middle Eastern restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Baklava — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18006)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for baklava (filo pastry with nuts and honey/syrup) used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent a standard mixed-nut baklava preparation consistent with the Sultan Palace restaurant description.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for baklava was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is estimated from the closest analogous food category: sweet pastry / filo-based pastry with sugar syrup, referencing Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which reports GI values for sweet baked goods and pastries in the medium range (50–65). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 16.05) / 100 ≈ 9.1, where carbohydrate per 30 g serving = 53.5 × 0.30 = 16.05 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (230 mg per 100 g) and high energy density
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit baklava to occasional small portions (one piece, ~30 g) due to its moderate sodium content and high caloric density; the butter and syrup components contribute to overall dietary sodium and energy load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (31.2 g per 100 g) and medium glycemic index (57)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat baklava as an occasional indulgence only; its high sugar load and medium GI (57) can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and a single 30 g piece delivers approximately 9.4 g of sugar and a glycemic load of ~9.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (7.4 g per 100 g), cholesterol (27 mg per 100 g), and high sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume baklava sparingly; its saturated fat from butter and cholesterol content, combined with high sugar, may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baklava is a rich, sweet pastry originating from the Ottoman Empire and now central to Turkish, Lebanese, Syrian, Greek, and broader Middle Eastern culinary traditions. It is made by layering thin sheets of filo (phyllo) pastry with generous amounts of chopped mixed nuts — typically pistachios, walnuts, or cashews — and binding the layers with clarified butter or ghee. After baking until golden and crisp, the pastry is saturated with a fragrant sugar syrup or honey, often perfumed with rose water or orange blossom water, which gives baklava its characteristic sticky sweetness and glossy appearance. The texture contrasts crisp, flaky pastry layers with a dense, moist nut filling. Nutritionally, baklava is energy-dense at approximately 428 kcal per 100 g, with significant carbohydrate (53.5 g, of which 31.2 g is sugar) and fat (22.8 g, including 7.4 g saturated fat) content. Its medium glycemic index (~57) means blood glucose rises moderately but the high sugar load makes portion control critical for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The nut content provides beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats, vitamin E, magnesium, and zinc. In Singapore, baklava is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants such as Sultan Palace at Boat Quay, typically sold by the piece or as a mixed platter. It is a popular dessert and gift item during Eid and other festive occasions across Muslim communities.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of baklava?
How much sugar is in baklava?
Where to buy baklava in Singapore?

confidence_reason: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy provides a direct baklava entry (filo pastry with nuts and syrup) that closely matches the traditional mixed-nut preparation described in the input. Confidence is medium rather than high because the Sultan Palace restaurant version may vary in nut composition, butter quantity, and syrup concentration relative to the USDA reference preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature rather than a direct measurement.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy baklava entry and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this food. GI value of 57 is an analogous estimate based on sweet filo-based pastry data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct baklava GI measurement was located in the University of Sydney GI Database at time of processing. The input record lists cuisine tags of Turkish, Kebab, and Shawarma; the Kebab and Shawarma tags are restaurant-level tags from field survey and do not reflect the food category of baklava itself. Baklava is classified as a dessert. The field survey price of SGD 14.90 likely reflects a multi-piece portion or platter rather than a single piece. Turkish cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); therefore only English and Malay local names are populated, with Arabic and Greek script aliases included in the romanized aliases section for retrieval breadth.
